name: Compassion (Karuna)
shortDescription: Karuna is the Buddhist concept of compassion, characterized by the wish for all beings to be free from suffering. It is a key aspect of Buddhist practice and a fundamental quality cultivated by Bodhisattvas.
description: Karuna, often translated as compassion, is a central concept in Buddhism, particularly within Mahayana Buddhism. It goes beyond simple empathy and involves actively wishing for the alleviation of suffering for all sentient beings. Karuna arises from the understanding of the interconnectedness of all beings and the recognition that suffering is a universal experience. It is not merely a feeling but a motivation for action, inspiring individuals to actively work towards reducing the pain and sorrow of others. Karuna is often paired with prajna (wisdom), as true compassion requires understanding the root causes of suffering and the means to overcome them. Bodhisattvas, beings who postpone their own enlightenment to help others, are particularly associated with the practice of karuna. Their unwavering dedication to the well-being of all beings embodies this compassionate ideal. The cultivation of karuna involves practices such as loving-kindness meditation (metta), which expands one's circle of care and concern to include all beings, without exception. Karuna is not simply a reaction to suffering but a proactive engagement with the world, driven by a deep sense of responsibility and the desire to create a more compassionate and just world. It is considered an essential element for achieving enlightenment-bodhi and is intricately linked to the concept of nirvana, the cessation of suffering. The active expression of Karuna is an integral part of Buddhist ethics and practice.

sources.title: The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching
sources.author: Thich Nhat Hanh

sources.title: In the Buddha's Words: An Anthology of Discourses from the Pali Canon
sources.author: Bhikkhu Bodhi
